title: Terry Fox: Marathon of Hope
dek: The young runner who turned an amputated leg and an unfinished road into a living cancer-research movement.
mortality_event: Diagnosed with osteosarcoma at 18, Terry Fox lost his right leg, ran the 1980 Marathon of Hope across Canada, and stopped near Thunder Bay after cancer spread to his lungs before his death in 1981.

title: Virginia Hall: The Limping Lady
dek: The spy who turned a wooden leg, a radio, and a network into Allied resistance.
mortality_event: After losing her lower left leg in a hunting accident, Virginia Hall used a wooden prosthetic named Cuthbert while serving with SOE and OSS in occupied France, evading the Gestapo and organizing Resistance networks.

title: Dith Pran: Killing Fields
dek: The Cambodian photojournalist who survived genocide and turned memory into witness.
mortality_event: Khmer Rouge forced labor, starvation, and escape through Cambodia’s killing fields before later public testimony.

title: Srinivasa Ramanujan: Mock Theta
dek: The dying mathematician whose last formulas kept opening doors.
mortality_event: Final illness and January 1920 letter to Hardy describing mock theta functions before his death at 32.

title: Irena Sendler: Jars of Names
dek: The Polish social worker who helped smuggle children from the Warsaw ghetto — and hid their real names in jars.
mortality_event: Gestapo arrest, torture, and death sentence in 1943 before Żegota bribed a guard and she survived underground.

title: Ernest Shackleton: Endurance After Ice
dek: The Antarctic explorer whose failed crossing became one of history’s clearest studies in leadership: when Endurance sank, he brought every man home alive.
mortality_event: Endurance was trapped, crushed, and sank in Antarctic pack ice; Shackleton led the survival effort through Elephant Island, the James Caird crossing, and rescue of the crew.

sources.name: Royal Museums Greenwich Death and Legacy

title: Paul Kalanithi: Breath Becomes Air
dek: Stanford neurosurgeon and writer whose posthumous memoir turned a terminal diagnosis into a lucid witness on medicine, mortality, fatherhood, and meaning.
mortality_event: Diagnosed with stage IV metastatic lung cancer at 36 near the end of neurosurgery training; died in 2015 while writing When Breath Becomes Air.

title: Flannery O'Connor: Lupus and Grace
dek: The Southern Gothic writer who made a shortened life speak in hard comic grace.
mortality_event: Diagnosed with lupus in 1950 — the disease that had killed her father — O'Connor spent her final fourteen years writing major fiction from Andalusia farm before dying at 39.

title: Mary Anning: Stone and Light
dek: The fossil hunter who survived lightning and taught science to read deep time.
mortality_event: Survived a lightning strike as an infant, then spent a short life uncovering extinct worlds from the Lyme Regis cliffs before dying of breast cancer at 47.

title: John Milton: Darkness Visible
dek: The blind poet who dictated Paradise Lost after sight failed.
mortality_event: Became totally blind by about 1652, then dictated Paradise Lost and other late works.

title: Jacques Lusseyran: Light in Darkness
dek: Blind French Resistance organizer, Buchenwald survivor, and author of And There Was Light.
mortality_event: Blinded in childhood, then arrested for Resistance work and deported to Buchenwald before surviving to write and teach about inner light.

title: Maya Angelou: Caged Bird Voice
dek: The poet who turned years of silence into a public language of survival.
mortality_event: Childhood trauma and years of mutism before literature, mentorship, memoir, and civil-rights witness returned her voice to the world.

title: Wilma Rudolph: The Girl Who Outran the Brace
dek: A child marked by polio became the first American woman to win three track-and-field gold medals at one Olympics.
mortality_event: Childhood polio and illness impaired her left leg before she learned to walk, run, and win three gold medals at Rome 1960.

title: The Man Who Moved After Stillness
dek: American actor and disability advocate who turned life after a 1995 spinal-cord injury into a public fight for paralysis research, access, and dignity.
mortality_event: 1995 equestrian accident causing paralysis from the neck down; afterward he used celebrity to build spinal-cord research and quality-of-life advocacy before his death in 2004.

title: The Tormented Light
dek: Dutch Post-Impressionist painter who created over 2,000 artworks in a decade, most in the final years before his death at 37.
mortality_event: 1888 ear mutilation and asylum commitment — between psychotic episodes, he painted The Starry Night, Irises, and 150 other masterpieces before his death in 1890.

title: Helen Keller: The Hand That Found Water
dek: A fever closed sight and sound; touch reopened the world.
mortality_event: At nineteen months, an acute illness left Helen Keller deafblind; at the water pump in 1887, language broke through by touch.

title: Roald Dahl: Crash and Chocolate
dek: The pilot who survived the desert and gave children dangerous stories.
mortality_event: 1940 RAF crash in the Libyan desert causing skull fracture, facial injuries, and temporary blindness.

title: George Orwell: The Throat of Truth
dek: The writer who survived a bullet through the throat and gave power a name.
mortality_event: Shot through the throat by a sniper in the Spanish Civil War in 1937; survived to write Animal Farm and Nineteen Eighty-Four before dying of tuberculosis in 1950.

title: The Merchant of Death
dek: Swedish chemist who invented dynamite, read his own premature obituary, and rewrote his legacy by founding the Nobel Prizes.
mortality_event: 1864 Heleneborg explosion killed brother Emil; 1888 premature obituary called him 'merchant of death' — twin hinges that led to the Nobel Prizes.

title: Blink Manuscript
dek: French Elle editor who dictated a landmark memoir one eyelid blink at a time.
mortality_event: 1995 stroke causing locked-in syndrome before he dictated The Diving Bell and the Butterfly by blinking.

title: The Night That Kept Speaking
dek: Romanian-born Jewish writer, Holocaust survivor, professor, and Nobel Peace Prize laureate whose Night made survivor testimony impossible to ignore.
mortality_event: Survived deportation as a teenager to Auschwitz and Buchenwald before writing Night and becoming a public witness against genocide.

title: The Captive Who Wrote the Knight
dek: Spanish soldier, captive, playwright, and novelist whose Don Quixote helped invent the modern novel.
mortality_event: Wounded at Lepanto in 1571 and held captive in Algiers for five years before Don Quixote.

title: The Man Who Wrote the Camps
dek: Russian novelist and historian who exposed the Soviet Gulag.
mortality_event: 1945 arrest, eight years in Soviet labour camps, internal exile, and cancer before the works that exposed the Gulag.

title: The Chemist Who Witnessed
dek: Italian Jewish chemist, Auschwitz survivor, and writer who turned survival into literature
mortality_event: Survived Auschwitz III-Monowitz (1944–45) before writing If This Is a Man and The Periodic Table
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.

title: Henri Matisse: The Second Life
dek: French modernist painter who turned illness into a final language of color and scissors.
mortality_event: Near-fatal 1941 surgery for duodenal cancer in Lyon, with complications and long confinement afterward.
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.

title: Harriet Tubman: The Road Back
dek: Underground Railroad conductor, abolitionist, Union scout, and suffragist.
mortality_event: Survived a severe head injury from an iron weight while enslaved, then escaped and repeatedly returned to rescue others.
sources: National Park Service — Harriet Tubman National Historical Park
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.

title: Time Under Shadow
dek: Theoretical physicist and public explainer who kept thinking about black holes, time, and the universe after a terminal diagnosis tried to close the future.
mortality_event: Diagnosed with motor neurone disease around his 21st birthday and given roughly two years to live before decades of later work on singularities, black holes, and public science.
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.

title: After the Raft
dek: Olympic runner, World War II airman, survivor, and public witness to endurance after the Pacific nearly erased him.
mortality_event: Survived a 1943 Pacific crash, 47 days adrift, and nearly two years as a Japanese prisoner of war before his later public testimony and forgiveness work.
sources: U.S. Air Force Veterans in Blue
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.

title: The Broken Mirror
dek: Mexican painter whose self-portraits made pain, identity, the body, and death modern art's evidence.
mortality_event: 1925 bus-trolley crash in Mexico City that shattered her body and led to a long recovery in which she began painting.

sources.name: National Museum of Women in the Arts

citable_summary: Frida Kahlo (1907–1954) was a Mexican painter whose self-portraits made pain, disability, identity, and death central evidence in modern art.
At age 18, Kahlo was badly injured in a bus-trolley crash in Mexico City; her long recovery helped turn painting from an interest into a life-defining practice.
This obituary comic frames the crash, the recovery bed, and the mirror as the conditions under which Kahlo began making the images that outlived her.
story_notes: Frida Kahlo did not originally set out to build a public mythology from pain. She was a young pre-medical student before the 1925 bus-trolley crash left her with spinal, pelvic, shoulder, and foot injuries that shaped the rest of her life.

The comic uses the mirror as a practical and symbolic object: while confined during recovery, Kahlo turned her own body and face into available subjects. That constraint became one of the sources of her visual language.

The sources for this page combine a general reference biography, a museum artist profile, an art-history biography, and an editorial account of how Kahlo used clothing, posture, and self-presentation around disability.
page_summaries: Opening page introducing Frida Kahlo and the broken-mirror frame of the comic.
Kahlo's early life and the turn from medical ambition toward a body marked by injury.
The 1925 collision becomes the central mortality event of the story.
Recovery narrows the room but gives the mirror and canvas new force.
Self-portraiture becomes a way to record pain, identity, and survival without softening them.
Kahlo's work grows from private injury into a public visual language.
The body remains damaged, but the paintings keep arguing with disappearance.
Closing page on what survived Kahlo: images that made pain impossible to ignore.
closing_line: The crash broke the plan. The mirror helped build the work that remains.

title: Meaning Under Ash
dek: Austrian neurologist, psychiatrist, Holocaust survivor, and founder of logotherapy.
mortality_event: Survived Nazi concentration camps including Theresienstadt, Auschwitz, Kaufering, and Türkheim.
citable_summary: Viktor Emil Frankl (1905–1997) was an Austrian neurologist and psychiatrist who survived Nazi concentration camps and later founded logotherapy.
Frankl was deported through Theresienstadt and Auschwitz, then sent to Kaufering and Türkheim, where he reconstructed ideas for his work while ill and imprisoned.
This obituary comic focuses on the loss of family and manuscript, then on Frankl's postwar reconstruction of meaning as clinical theory and testimony.
story_notes: Frankl's story is not simply a survival anecdote. Before deportation, he was already a psychiatrist concerned with suicide, depression, and the human need for meaning.
The comic follows the pressure placed on that work by the camps: family members were murdered, his manuscript was lost, and the question of meaning had to survive contact with catastrophe.

After liberation, Frankl returned to Vienna and wrote the work published in English as Man's Search for Meaning. The page's sources connect his institutional biography, reference biography, and the publication history of the book.
page_summaries: Opening page introducing Viktor Frankl and the search for meaning under catastrophe.
Frankl's early medical and psychiatric work before deportation.
The Nazi camps strip away family, home, and manuscript.
Auschwitz and transfer to forced labor camps become the story's central mortality pressure.
Illness and deprivation test whether meaning can remain more than an idea.
Frankl reconstructs thought under conditions designed to erase personhood.
Liberation leaves grief and the task of rebuilding the work.
Closing page on logotherapy and the book that carried the testimony forward.
closing_line: The camps took his family and a manuscript. The work he rebuilt is what remains.

title: Borrowed Time
dek: Russian novelist. Survivor of a staged execution.
mortality_event: 1849 staged execution / mock firing squad at Semyonovsky Square
citable_summary: Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoyevsky (1821–1881) was a Russian novelist whose 1849 mock execution and Siberian imprisonment became decisive facts in his later fiction.
Dostoyevsky was arrested over the Petrashevsky Circle, sentenced to death, taken to a firing-squad ritual, and reprieved only at the last moment before being sent to Siberia.
This obituary comic treats the reprieve as borrowed time: the later novels return again and again to freedom, guilt, punishment, faith, and life under extreme moral pressure.
story_notes: Dostoyevsky's near-execution was staged as punishment before exile, but he experienced it as an immediate confrontation with death. That moment became a recurring imaginative source in his mature work.
The comic follows the path from early literary promise to arrest, the square, the lowered guns, prison at Omsk, and the later novels that turned punishment into artistic material.

The source trail combines a reference account of the arrest, an essay on Dostoyevsky's letters and post-prison work, a public-domain text from his prison literature, and a Literary Hub essay on the mock execution's afterlife in the novels.
page_summaries: Opening page introducing Dostoyevsky and the idea of borrowed time.
Early life near suffering and institutions of poverty.
The young writer resists the uniform path expected of him.
Poor Folk opens the possibility of literary life.
Rooms, circles, and forbidden conversations draw state attention.
The arrest closes around the Petrashevsky Circle.
Semyonovsky Square becomes the stage for a firing-squad ritual.
The guns lower after a last-minute reprieve.
Life inside punishment replaces the expected death.
Omsk prison becomes the brutal school behind later fiction.
Books and memory survive the furnace of exile.
The soul goes on trial in the later novels.
St. Petersburg and 1881 frame the end of the life.
Closing page on the work that remains after borrowed time.
closing_line: The reprieve was one moment. The work was the rest of his life.
